rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, July 10, 2024, line 1, DEFENDANT TIJI THOMAS MOTION AND NOTICE OF HEARING AND MPA FOR MOTION TO COMPEL AND MOTION FOR SANCTIONS (TENTATIVE RULING, PART 2 OF 2) For the 9:00 a.m. Discovery calendar, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link (DISCOVERY, DEPARTMENT 302 DAILY AT 9:00 A.M.), or dial the corresponding number and use the meeting ID, and password for Discovery Department 302. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: IN RE: YOLANDA CONN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion And Notice Of Hearing And Mpa For Motion To Compel And Motion For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, July 10, 2024, line 1, DEFENDANT TIJI THOMAS Motion And Notice Of Hearing And Mpa For Motion To Compel And Motion For Sanctions (tentative ruing part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion to compel GRANTED. Despite the various revisions throughout the process, it is still unclear which contracts Plaintiff alleges she had with Defendant Thomas or explained how Defendant Thomas breached any alleged contract. From the opposition (but not the operative complaint), Plaintiff appears to allege that Defendant Thomas engaged in tortious conduct to induce her to form contracts with other entities, including the various MOBE entities. If this is the case, Plaintiff should say so. That would allow this Defendant to move to strike the breach of contract claims against him. Alternatively, Plaintiff could voluntarily dismiss the contract claims against Defendant Thomas. If this is not the correct reading of the allegations, Plaintiff must specifically identify which contracts she alleges existed between her and Defendant Thomas, and then articulate how he breached those contracts. Further responses - or a withdrawal of the breach of contract claim - due by July 24, 2024 (unless otherwise agreed by the parties). The request for monetary sanctions is GRANTED in the amount of $3,800 ($475 x 8 hours for drafting the motion and meeting and conferring) payable by the same date. The request for evidentiary sanctions is GRANTED in part. Unless Plaintiff timely complies with this order and identifies the contract(s) she alleges existed between her and Defendant Thomas, and then explains how he breached those contract(s), she will be prohibited from introduce evidence at summary judgment or trial that would go to the issue of breach of contract against Defendant Thomas. (end of tentative ruling part 1, see part 2) = (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: TALIA KENNEDY VS. META PLATFORMS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Partially Renewed Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents (Set One) To Meta Platforms, Inc. And Request For Monetary Sanctions In The Amount Of $5,760.00 Against Said Defendant And Its Counsel, Jointly And Severally; Dec Of William Reed
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, July 10, 2024, line 2, PLAINTIFF TALIA KENNEDY Partially Renewed Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents (Set One) To Meta Platforms, Inc. And Request For Monetary Sanctions In The Amount Of $5,760.00 Against Said Defendant And Its Counsel, Jointly And Severally; Dec Of William Reed (tentativer ruling part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion to compel DENIED without prejudice. The Court of Appeal held in Wellpoint that if a defendant employer seeks to show that "it investigated an employee's complaint and took appropriate action to the findings of the investigation, then it will have put the adequacy of the investigation directly at issue, and cannot stand on the attorney-client privilege or work product doctrine to preclude a thorough examination of its adequacy." 59 Cal.App.4th at 128. However, the conflict-of-interest investigation on which Meta said it based its decision to terminate Plaintiff is different from an investigation into Plaintiff's complaints of harassment that might support such a defense. Rather, it was an investigation into alleged violations of policy akin to the misconduct investigation discussed in Kaiser, where the Court of Appeal upheld an assertion of privilege. 66 Cal.App.4th at 1225-26. The analytical difference is that evaluating the "adequacy of the investigation" is required for a harassment defense but is not required to prove that an employee violated a policy. The first amended privilege log (excluding the color-coded components, which I understand were produced) includes sufficient information (including authors and recipients) for Plaintiff to identify any specific materials for which she challenges the assertion of privilege or protection. However, the current motion and related meet-and-confer correspondence reflect a categorical argument (produce everything because privilege/protection are waived) as opposed to specific challenges to various documents. Because Plaintiff's categorical argument is not persuasive, the motion is denied without prejudice so that the parties can address specific documents. In camera review appears premature. Plaintiff should identify specific documents she believes are discoverable so that Meta can respond to specific documents. Once there is such a record, in camera review may be appropriate. Sanctions are DENIED. (End of part 1 see part 2) = (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: TALIA KENNEDY VS. META PLATFORMS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, July 10, 2024, line 2, DEFENDANT ETHAN LUBKA, IAN LAIKS, META PLATFORMS, INC. (F/K/A FACEBOOK, INC.) Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion for protective order GRANTED. CCP 2030.040(b) permits a protective order based on an argument that the number of specially prepared interrogatories is unwarranted and provides that the burden of justifying excessive interrogatories is on the propounding party. Additionally, CRC 3.1345(b) does not require a separate statement where there has been no response to the requests for discovery. This makes sense here because Meta has not yet made particularized objections to any request but instead seeks to hold Plaintiff to her burden to justify serving interrogatories beyond the presumptive limit, so the Court does not need a separate statement to understand the relationship between the requests and the objections. While some of the proposed special interrogatories appear facially proper, as Meta points out, they may be duplicative of other discovery devices such as form interrogatories. The Civil Discovery Act requires that courts restrict discovery that is cumulative or duplicative. CCP 2019.030. As such, faced with a motion for protective order under CCP 2030.040(b), Plaintiff had the obligation to show that the special interrogatories are proper - including that they are not unreasonably cumulative of other discovery. Plaintiff has not met her burden here because the record does not include sufficient information about how these proposed interrogatories are meaningfully different from other discovery already propounded. (end of tentativer ruling part 1 see part 2) = (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF TEOFILO FERRER ET AL VS. SUTTER HEALTH, A BUSINESS ENTITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Wednesday, July 10, 2024, Line 7. DEFENDANT SUTTER HEALTH AND CALIFORNIA PACIFIC MEDICAL CENTER's DEMURRER to 2ND Amended COMPLAINT. Defendants' demurrer to the second amended complaint's (SAC) medical battery count is overruled. For the pleading stage, the SAC adequately alleges medical battery - that a nurse gave decedent a third drug dose at 3:20 p.m. without his consent and without a doctor's order. (SAC 9:2-10:16; CACI 530A.) Defendants point to another SAC passage which says the nurse's note regarding the 3:20 dose "implies she received a verbal order from" a doctor. (Memo. 4:22-25; SAC 6:2-6.) This can be explored in discovery. In any event, a complaint "may plead in the alternative;" factually and/or legally inconsistent allegations are permissible. (Adams v. Paul (1995) 11 Cal.4th 583, 593.) For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: JULIA MALDONADO VS. DELTA DENTAL OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrog, Set One To Deft Delta Dental
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, July 10, 2024, line 3, PLAINTIFF JULIA MALDONADO Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrog, Set One To Deft Delta Dental (tentative ruling part 1 see part 2) Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion to compel GRANTED. Defendant could have brought a motion for protective order under CCP 2030.040(b) (as their colleagues did in the matter heard at Line 2), which would have required Plaintiff to justify the excessive interrogatories. Defendant did not take this approach but instead made generalized objections. By taking this approach, Defendant has the burden of justifying its objections. Williams v. Sup. Ct (2017) 3 Cal.5th 531, 541. Burdens matter, and here Defendant did not meet its burden of supporting the objections. For example, the responses consistently object "to the extent" each interrogatory is duplicative of prior interrogatory but do not identify potentially duplicative interrogatories or other discovery devices. Based on the separate statement, Rogs 54 - 56 appear duplicative of Rogs 3-5, but that is not clear in the formal discovery responses. Plaintiff and the Court should not be required to rely on motion papers to understand routine objections like this. In another example, for Rog 37, while Plaintiff may have testified at deposition that she only raised a complaint against one person, Defendant's argument that it should not respond to other complaints about people mistreating Plaintiff is not well taken because Plaintiff cannot have known whether coworkers had complained on Plaintiff's behalf or whether Defendant investigated other concerns. No later than July 24, 2024 (unless otherwise agreed), Defendant must provide updated responses to the interrogatories. Any objections must be sufficiently specific that Plaintiff (or a reviewing court) can evaluate their merit without having to rely on argument in motion papers. Sanctions GRANTED in the amount of $5,000, reduced from the request to account for the fact that some of the interrogatories appear duplicative and so further meeting and conferring could have resolved many of these issues. (end of tentative ruling part 1 see part 2)

rulings.Case Title: JULIA MALDONADO VS. DELTA DENTAL OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatories Employment-Law, Set One, To Defendant Delta Dental Of California
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, July 10, 2024, line 3, PLAINTIFF JULIA MALDONADO Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatories Employment-Law, Set One, To Defendant Delta Dental Of California Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion to compel GRANTED in part and DENIED in part. Section 1(c) of the Instructions to Form Interrogatories makes clear that an answering party retains the right to make any objection to form interrogatories. Considering the proffer on the scope of Defendant's operations in California, the objection based on scope is sustained. However, Defendant must still answer the interrogatory to the extent possible - including the extent to which it does not object. CCP 2030.220(b). Thus, Defendant must provide complete information for those actions to which it does not object. If Plaintiff remains unsatisfied with the response, Plaintiff may meet and confer and then file another motion to compel. Any such motion after amendment will allow the Court to evaluate whether the limited response is sufficient. Sanctions DENIED. Further meet and confer could have resolved this dispute. For the 9:00 a.m. Discovery calendar, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link (DISCOVERY, DEPARTMENT 302 DAILY AT 9:00 A.M.), or dial the corresponding number and use the meeting ID, and password for Discovery Department 302. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (302/JPT)
